essayHeadings: The Ascetic Who Holds the Cosmos
Where the Scriptures Speak of Him
Reading His Iconography
The Five Acts and the Many Forms
Mondays, Shivaratri, and the Month of Shravan
The Living Stotra Tradition
essay: Ash on the body, a crescent in the matted hair, a serpent coiled at the throat, and eyes half-closed in meditation on the snowbound peak of Kailasa. Shiva is the great ascetic, yet he is also the householder wedded to Parvati and father to Ganesha and Kartikeya. Hindus call him Mahadeva, the supreme god, and in the Shaiva understanding he is the absolute reality from which creation rises and into which it dissolves. Destroyer is the common gloss, but it misleads. He dissolves so that the next cycle may begin.

The Shiva Purana is the central text, narrating his marriage, his children, and the worship of the linga across its many sections. The Linga Purana expands on the formless pillar of light through which Brahma and Vishnu first failed to measure his limits. Older still is the Rudram, the Sri Rudram of the Krishna Yajurveda, chanted at every major Shaiva rite, whose Namakam invokes Rudra by a hundred names and whose Chamakam asks for the things a life needs. Adi Shankaracharya wrote of him too, in hymns like the Dakshinamurti Stotra.

Look closely and every object on his body carries weight. The third eye, opened, reduced Kama the god of desire to ash. The Ganga descends through his hair, broken by those locks so the earth could bear her fall. The trident, the trishula, names the three gunas and the three states of waking, dream, and sleep. The damaru drum beats out the rhythm of time. The serpent Vasuki rests unafraid at his neck, and the tiger skin beneath him marks the conqueror of the animal nature.

The Shaiva tradition speaks of the Panchakritya, his five acts: creation, preservation, dissolution, concealment, and grace. He takes many forms to perform them. As Nataraja he dances the Ananda Tandava within a ring of fire, a form the Chidambaram temple enshrines. As Ardhanarishvara he is half Parvati, the union of consciousness and energy in one body. The Linga Purana counts his Jyotirlinga manifestations, the twelve self-luminous shrines from Somnath to Kedarnath where devotees say he is present in his fullest measure.

Monday belongs to Shiva, and many keep a fast on that day. Maha Shivaratri, the great night of Shiva in the dark fortnight of Phalguna, is his foremost festival: devotees stay awake, bathe the linga with milk and water, and offer bilva leaves through the night watches. The month of Shravan in the monsoon draws pilgrims who carry Ganga water on foot to pour over the linga, the kanwar yatra. Pradosha, the twilight of the thirteenth lunar day, is held especially dear by those who worship him.

Few hymns are recited as widely as the Shiv Tandav Stotram, attributed to Ravana, whose thundering meter imitates the god's own dance. The Lingashtakam praises the linga in eight verses for daily worship, and the Rudrashtakam from Tulsidas's retelling begins with the line that asks for the indescribable. The Bilvashtakam accompanies the offering of bilva leaves, and Shankaracharya's Dakshinamurti and Shivananda Lahari carry his philosophy in verse. Together these stotras form the sound by which households and temples call him near.

essayHeadings: Rising from the Churned Ocean
Her Vedic and Puranic Sources
Lotus, Gold, and the Four Hands
The Eight Lakshmis and Her Consort
Diwali, Fridays, and the Harvest Months
Hymns That Invite Her In
essay: When the gods and demons churned the milk ocean for the nectar of immortality, fourteen treasures surfaced, and among them rose a goddess seated on a lotus, garlanded and radiant, who chose Vishnu as her lord. This is Lakshmi, Sri, the goddess of fortune, abundance, and well-being. Her name comes from the Sanskrit for an aim or mark, the goal a life moves toward. She is not wealth as mere coin, but the wholeness that lets a household, a kingdom, or a body flourish. Where she settles, prosperity follows; where she leaves, decay sets in.

Her oldest praise is the Sri Suktam, a khila appendix to the Rigveda, which calls upon the golden-hued goddess and asks her never to depart. The Bhagavata Purana and the Vishnu Purana tell of her birth from the churning and her bond with Vishnu through his every descent, as Sita beside Rama and Rukmini beside Krishna. The Devi Mahatmya, set within the Markandeya Purana, honors her among the great goddesses. Adi Shankaracharya's Kanakadhara Stotram recounts how his praise of her once rained gold upon a poor woman's house.

Lakshmi sits or stands upon a fully opened lotus, and lotuses bloom in two of her hands, the flower that grows from mud yet stays unstained. Gold coins stream from a lower palm turned downward in the gesture of giving. Her fourth hand may rest in abhaya, the sign that dispels fear. Two elephants often flank her, lifting pitchers to bathe her, the Gaja Lakshmi form that signals royal power and rain-fed plenty. Red and gold clothe her, and the owl at her side, her vahana, watches through the dark.

Tradition groups her into the Ashta Lakshmi, the eight forms that govern distinct kinds of abundance: Adi the primeval, Dhana of money, Dhanya of grain, Gaja of cattle and royalty, Santana of progeny, Vira or Dhairya of courage, Vijaya of victory, and Vidya, sometimes counted, of learning. As consort she is inseparable from Vishnu, the active force to his stillness, and in the Sri Vaishnava school she is the compassionate mediator who carries the devotee's plea to him. She is worshipped beside Ganesha at the start of ventures.

Diwali is her night above all others. On the new-moon darkness of Kartika, households clean every corner, light rows of lamps, draw rangoli at the threshold, and open doors and windows so she may enter and stay. Friday is her weekday, kept by many with a vow and a recitation of her hymns. Kojagiri Purnima, the full moon of Ashvin, is when she is said to roam at night asking who is awake to receive her. The harvest months and the start of the business year are marked with her worship and fresh account books.

The Sri Suktam remains the foundation, chanted in temples and homes during her puja. Shankaracharya's Kanakadhara Stotram is recited for relief from want, verse by verse describing the play of her sidelong glance. The Mahalakshmi Ashtakam, drawn from the Padma Purana and spoken by Indra, praises her in eight stanzas and is among the most common Diwali recitations. The Lakshmi Chalisa, in forty Hindi verses, carries her worship into everyday devotion, and the Sri Lakshmi Sahasranama lists her thousand names for those who keep longer rites.

essayHeadings: The Prince of Ayodhya
Valmiki and the Tellings After Him
Bow, Blue Form, and the Faithful Few
The Arc of the Ramayana
Rama Navami and the Days of His Story
Stotras of the Name
essay: On the eve of his coronation, the heir to Ayodhya was sent into fourteen years of forest exile by a promise his father could not break, and he went without a word of complaint. That single act, obedience held above his own crown, is why Hindus remember Rama as maryada purushottama, the perfect man who never strayed from dharma. The seventh avatar of Vishnu, born to King Dasharatha to rid the earth of the demon Ravana, he is worshipped not for cosmic might but for a life lived rightly under impossible weight.

Valmiki's Ramayana is the source, the adi-kavya or first poem, told across seven kandas from the Bala Kanda of his boyhood to the Uttara Kanda of his later reign. Each book carries a stage of the story: the Ayodhya Kanda of exile, the Aranya Kanda of the forest, the Kishkindha Kanda of the monkey alliance, the Sundara Kanda of Hanuman's leap, and the Yuddha Kanda of the war. Tulsidas retold it in Awadhi as the Ramcharitmanas, and the Bhagavata Purana and Adhyatma Ramayana give their own devotional accounts.

Rama is shown dark-blue or green of complexion like Vishnu, a tall figure holding the great bow Kodanda, often with a quiver of arrows at his back. He stands flanked by his constant companions: Sita his wife at his side, his brother Lakshmana behind, and Hanuman kneeling at his feet, palms joined. The tilaka marks his brow and a simple crown or matted forest locks may rest on his head depending on the episode shown. Where his image stands, that group of four is the heart of the scene.

The poem's arc runs through loss and recovery. Won in marriage by stringing Shiva's bow, Rama is exiled with Sita and Lakshmana; in the forest Ravana abducts Sita to Lanka. Hanuman finds her, leaps the ocean, and the monkey army builds a bridge of stones. The war that follows ends with Ravana's fall and Sita's rescue, and the three return to Ayodhya for the coronation that exile had postponed. The Sundara Kanda, the fifth book of Hanuman's deeds, is recited on its own for courage and protection.

Rama Navami, the ninth day of the bright fortnight of Chaitra, marks his birth at noon, and temples cradle a small idol while devotees fast, read the Ramayana, and sing his praises. The nine nights of Chaitra Navaratri lead up to it. Vijayadashami, Dussehra, celebrates his victory over Ravana, when effigies of the ten-headed demon are burned across north India. Diwali itself is tied to his homecoming, the lamps of Ayodhya lit to welcome the returning king after fourteen years away.

The Ramraksha Stotra, composed by the sage Budhakaushika, is the armor of Rama, recited for protection through the day and night. The Ram Stuti, including Tulsidas's well-loved Shri Ramachandra Kripalu, sets his praise to melody for evening prayer. Hanuman's worship runs alongside his own, the Hanuman Chalisa and the Bajrang Baan sung to invoke the devotee who served him best. The Aditya Hridayam, taught to Rama on the battlefield by the sage Agastya before he faced Ravana, hymns the sun and is read for strength.

essayHeadings: Goddess of the Word
From the Vedic River to the Puranas
The Veena, the White Swan, the Book
Speech, Knowledge, and Her Companions
Vasant Panchami and the Days of Learning
Verses for the Tongue
essay: Before she was the goddess of learning, Saraswati was a river. The Rigveda sings of her as the mightiest of streams, ambitame naditame devitame, best of mothers, best of rivers, best of goddesses, a torrent that nourished the early Vedic world before it vanished underground. From the flowing of water the tradition drew the flowing of speech and thought, and she became Vagdevi, the goddess of the spoken word, of music, memory, and every form of knowledge. Students bow to her before the first lesson, and musicians before the first note.

Her roots are the deepest of the four, set in the Rigveda where she is both river and inspirer of hymns, and in the goddess Vac of the Vak Sukta who declares herself the source of all speech. Later texts personify her fully: the Brahma Vaivarta Purana and the Devi Bhagavata Purana name her among the great goddesses and consort of Brahma the creator. The Devi Mahatmya of the Markandeya Purana honors her as Mahasaraswati, one of the three forms of the supreme goddess who slays the demons of ignorance and disorder.

She is dressed in white, the color of pure light untouched by passion, seated on a white lotus or a rock by flowing water. Four hands carry the marks of her gift: the veena she plays for the music of the universe, a string of rudraksha or crystal beads for meditation, a manuscript or palm-leaf book for the scriptures and grammar, and sometimes a water pot. The swan, hamsa, rests beside her, the bird said to separate milk from water as discernment separates truth from falsehood. A peacock may also attend her.

Saraswati governs the realms a mind reaches into: vidya the learning, vac the speech, kala the arts, and smriti the memory that holds them. As Brahma's consort she is the knowledge by which he shapes creation, for the tradition holds that nothing can be made without first being known and named. She is invoked at the laying of foundations of study, and in the Navaratri of autumn she is worshipped on the later days when books and instruments are placed before her and left unread in rest. Her grace is sought against dullness and the stammer of fear.

Vasant Panchami, the fifth day of the bright fortnight of Magha, is her day, falling as spring begins. Devotees dress in yellow for the mustard fields in bloom, place pens, books, and instruments at her feet, and many teach young children their first letters that morning, the vidyarambham. In the south and east her worship peaks on the last three days of Sharada Navaratri, when tools of every trade are gathered for the Ayudha Puja and worshipped before the festival's close, then taken up again on Vijayadashami.

The Saraswati Vandana, the prayer that opens with ya kundendu tushara hara dhavala, is recited in schools and assemblies across India before study begins. The Saraswati Stotram, attributed in part to the sage Agastya, asks her for wisdom and clear speech. The Saraswati Chalisa carries her praise in forty verses for daily recitation, and the Saraswati Ashtakam offers her eight stanzas of homage. Students keep these on their lips during exams and at the start of any course, asking the goddess of the word to loosen the tongue and steady the mind.
